name: Japan
tagline: Ancient forests, neon cities, and food that borders on spiritual.
description: Japan holds the rare ability to be both overwhelming and deeply calming — a place where a centuries-old temple sits two blocks from a convenience store stocking the best egg salad sandwich you've ever had. Every region has its own personality, dialect, and obsession, and the sheer density of things worth caring about never exhausts you.

cities.name: Tokyo
cities.notes: Bottomless city — Shibuya's scramble, Yanaka's old alleys, Shimokitazawa's vintage shops. You could spend a month and only scratch it.

cities.name: Kyoto
cities.notes: Temple walks at dawn before the tour groups arrive. Fushimi Inari at night is something else entirely.

cities.name: Osaka
cities.notes: The food capital: takoyaki, okonomiyaki, kushikatsu. People here will argue that Tokyo can't cook.

cities.name: Hakone
cities.notes: Ryokan with an onsen overlooking Fuji on a clear morning — one of those experiences that becomes a benchmark for everything after.

food.name: Ramen
food.notes: Tonkotsu in Fukuoka, shoyu in Tokyo, miso in Sapporo — each region considers its version the only version worth eating.

food.name: Omakase sushi
food.notes: The counter, the chef, no menu. You trust the process and the fish is whatever came in that morning.

food.name: Kaiseki
food.notes: A multi-course meal that moves through the seasons in small, precise bites. More ceremony than food, in the best sense.

food.name: Takoyaki
food.notes: Octopus balls, bonito flakes, mayo, sauce — eaten standing outside a stall in Osaka at 10pm.

food.name: Onigiri
food.notes: The convenience store kind is genuinely perfect. Tuna mayo, pickled plum, salmon. Breakfast of champions.

tips: Get a Suica or IC card at the airport — it works on trains, buses, and most convenience stores nationwide.
Convenience store food (7-Eleven, FamilyMart, Lawson) is sincerely excellent. Don't skip it.
Book popular restaurants weeks in advance, especially in Kyoto. Walk-ins at the best places are rare.
Cash is still king in rural areas and smaller restaurants. Keep some on you.

name: Italy
tagline: Where every meal is a small argument about the right way to do things.
description: Italy isn't one country, it's twenty — each region fiercely convinced that its pasta shape, its olive oil, its grandmother's method is the definitive one. There's something infectious about a place that takes beauty and food this seriously, where a Sunday lunch can last until dusk and nobody considers that excessive.

cities.name: Rome
cities.notes: Two thousand years of history stacked on top of itself. You turn a corner and walk into the Pantheon. Negroni sbagliato at dusk on a piazza.

cities.name: Bologna
cities.notes: The real food capital of Italy, and they'll tell you so. Every ragù, every tortellini, every mortadella here is the original.

cities.name: Naples
cities.notes: Chaotic, loud, and home to the best pizza on earth. The sfogliatelle from the bar on the corner at 7am is not optional.

cities.name: Sicily
cities.notes: Arancini, cannoli, ancient Greek temples overlooking the sea. A separate universe from the north, and proudly so.

food.name: Cacio e pepe
food.notes: Three ingredients: pasta, pecorino, black pepper. The simplicity is deceptive — getting the emulsification right takes practice.

food.name: Neapolitan pizza
food.notes: Soft, slightly charred, wet in the middle. Margherita or marinara. Everything else is a compromise.

food.name: Bistecca alla Fiorentina
food.notes: A T-bone from a Chianina cow, cooked over charcoal, served rare. Florence does one thing with meat and it's this.

food.name: Gelato
food.notes: The real thing is denser and less sweet than ice cream. Pistachio from Sicily, stracciatella in Rome — avoid the places with fluorescent towers.

food.name: Aperitivo
food.notes: Not just a drink — a ritual. Negroni or Aperol Spritz with olives and chips at 6pm before dinner at 9.

tips: Eat where the locals eat — away from the tourist center, no English menus outside, tables full at 1pm.
Lunch is the main meal. A three-course lunch at a trattoria is often better value and quality than dinner.
Churches require covered shoulders and knees. Carry a scarf.
The trains connecting smaller towns are scenic and cheap. Don't only stay in the major cities.

name: Taiwan
tagline: Night markets, mountain hot springs, and the best bubble tea you'll ever have.
description: Taiwan packs an improbable amount into a small island: dense, exciting cities alongside gorges and mountain ranges that feel untouched, all surrounded by a food culture that punches far above its weight. People here are legendarily kind to visitors, which makes an already great trip feel even warmer.

cities.name: Taipei
cities.notes: Walkable, friendly, cheap to eat well. Daan District for coffee shops, Xinyi for skyline views from the 101, Jiufen for nostalgia.

cities.name: Tainan
cities.notes: The oldest city on the island and the best food city in Taiwan — a high bar. Every alley has a stall that's been doing one dish for 40 years.

cities.name: Taroko Gorge
cities.notes: A marble canyon with turquoise rivers. One of the most dramatic pieces of landscape in Asia.

cities.name: Jiufen
cities.notes: Hillside tea houses with red lanterns and mountain views over the sea. The inspiration behind a certain Miyazaki film.

food.name: Beef noodle soup
food.notes: Braised beef, hand-pulled noodles, rich spiced broth. Taipei's unofficial national dish — every restaurant has its own version.

food.name: Scallion pancake
food.notes: Flaky, layered, eaten from a street cart with egg folded in. Morning staple.

food.name: Stinky tofu
food.notes: The smell is a test. The taste is deeply savory and fermented. Night market essential.

food.name: Bubble milk tea
food.notes: The original. Chun Shui Tang in Taichung claims to have invented it. Creamy, cold, chewy tapioca — no comparison to what the world exports.

food.name: Oyster omelette
food.notes: Oysters, sweet potato starch, egg, sauce. Found at every night market. Best eaten standing up.

tips: Get an EasyCard on arrival — it covers the MRT, buses, and YouBike rentals across the country.
Night markets are dinner. Raohe, Shilin, Ningxia in Taipei — arrive hungry at 7pm.
The east coast by scooter or train is worth the extra days. Hualien to Taitung is spectacular.
Most things are cheaper than you expect. Budget well on food and the rest takes care of itself.

name: Ireland
tagline: Green cliffs, peat fires, and pubs that feel like someone's living room.
description: Ireland earns its reputation for landscapes and hospitality — the west coast in particular has a wild, untouched quality that makes everywhere else feel manicured by comparison. The pub culture isn't just drinking; it's the social infrastructure of the country, where strangers start talking and somehow three hours pass.

cities.name: Dublin
cities.notes: More literary and historically dense than its party-city reputation suggests. Trinity College, the Book of Kells, Georgian squares, and yes — Guinness.

cities.name: Galway
cities.notes: The west coast's beating heart. Street musicians, oyster festivals, a gateway to Connemara's emptiness.

cities.name: Dingle
cities.notes: A peninsula of extraordinary beauty. Dolphins in the harbor, fish chowder at a pub fire, stone walls across the hills.

cities.name: Kilkenny
cities.notes: Medieval city with a castle, a medieval mile, and some of the best craft beer in the country.

food.name: Irish stew
food.notes: Lamb, potatoes, onions, slow-cooked. Simple and deeply satisfying after a day on the cliffs.

food.name: Full Irish breakfast
food.notes: Rashers, sausages, black pudding, white pudding, eggs, beans, toast, soda bread. The only appropriate response to a cold morning.

food.name: Seafood chowder
food.notes: Cream-based, loaded with fish and mussels. Best found in a pub on the west coast with soda bread on the side.

food.name: Guinness
food.notes: Genuinely different on home soil — creamier, fresher, poured properly with the two-minute settle. Worth the trip alone.

tips: Rent a car for the west coast — public transport won't take you where you want to go.
Weather changes hourly. Layers, a waterproof, and no rigid plans.
The Wild Atlantic Way is a coastal drive from Donegal to Cork. Even a section of it is worth it.
Pub hours matter. Last orders around 11:30pm — arrive for a pint and a conversation, not just a drink.

name: Greece
tagline: White walls, blue domes, and olive oil on everything.
description: Greece delivers on its promise — the light really is different, the sea really is that color, and eating a simple grilled fish beside the Aegean at sunset really does make you question every life choice that kept you elsewhere. The islands each have their own character, and the mainland is consistently underrated.

cities.name: Athens
cities.notes: The Acropolis at golden hour is one of the great urban views on earth. The neighborhoods below — Monastiraki, Psiri, Exarcheia — reward walking.

cities.name: Santorini
cities.notes: The cliché exists for a reason. Oia at sunset, caldera views, wine made from vines grown in volcanic ash.

cities.name: Thessaloniki
cities.notes: Greece's second city and arguably better for food. Bougatsa for breakfast, meze by the sea, Byzantine churches in every other block.

cities.name: Crete
cities.notes: The largest island: ancient Minoan palaces, gorge hikes through Samaria, and a food culture distinct from the rest of the country.

food.name: Souvlaki
food.notes: Grilled pork or chicken on a skewer with pita, tomato, onion, tzatziki. The best fast food in Europe, eaten standing on the street.

food.name: Spanakopita
food.notes: Spinach and feta in flaky phyllo. A good version — still warm from the bakery — is something else.

food.name: Fresh grilled octopus
food.notes: Charred over coals, drizzled with olive oil and lemon. Best eaten at a harbor-side table where they're drying on the line outside.

food.name: Greek salad
food.notes: No lettuce. Tomatoes, cucumber, olives, capers, a slab of feta, olive oil. The ingredients are the point.

food.name: Loukoumades
food.notes: Honey-drenched fried dough, dusted with cinnamon. Street food in Athens, ancient as the city itself.

tips: Visit the islands in shoulder season (May, September) — same weather, half the crowds and prices.
Ferry between islands rather than flying — it's part of the experience and lets you see the sea.
Lunch is the main meal. A proper taverna lunch with carafe wine costs a fraction of an equivalent dinner.
Most ancient sites open early and are quieter in the first hour after opening.

name: France
tagline: A country that treats a good lunch like a sacred obligation.
description: France is exasperating and magnificent in equal measure — it sets the standard for food, wine, art, and architecture, and it knows it. But beyond the postcard Paris, there's Burgundy wine country in autumn, the rocky Brittany coast, Lyon's bouchons, and Provence in lavender season, each deserving its own trip.

cities.name: Paris
cities.notes: Overwhelming and worth it. Treat it like a neighborhood city — pick an arrondissement and walk until something stops you.

cities.name: Lyon
cities.notes: The real food capital of France, and they have the bouchons to prove it. Quenelles, andouillette, praline tart — come hungry.

cities.name: Marseille
cities.notes: Gritty, beautiful, misunderstood. Bouillabaisse by the Vieux-Port and the calanques a short drive south.

cities.name: Bordeaux
cities.notes: Wine country, grand architecture, and a regenerated waterfront. A city that aged better than most.

food.name: Croissant
food.notes: The standard for pastry everywhere. A great one — laminated properly, shatteringly flaky — is not the same as what you get outside France.

food.name: Steak frites
food.notes: Bavette or entrecôte, cooked bleu or saignant, with real frites and a good Béarnaise. A bistro institution.

food.name: Bouillabaisse
food.notes: Marseille's saffron-scented fish stew, with rouille-slathered croutons and whatever came off the boats that morning.

food.name: Cheese course
food.notes: A rolling cart, thirty options, a sommelier to guide you through it. The French take this seriously, and rightly so.

food.name: Tarte Tatin
food.notes: Upside-down caramelized apple tart. The kind of dessert that makes you wonder why anyone does anything else.

tips: Learn a few words. Bonjour, s'il vous plaît, merci — the reception you get changes immediately.
Shops close for lunch. Banks, pharmacies, bakeries — 12:30 to 2pm is sacrosanct.
The regional trains (TER) are great for getting into the countryside without renting a car.
A picnic from a market, a boulangerie, and a fromagerie will beat most restaurant meals for half the price.

name: Vietnam
tagline: A thousand-mile country where every region insists its food is best.
description: Vietnam's length means you're essentially traveling through multiple countries — the restrained, refined north feels nothing like the sweeter, more exuberant south, with Hue and Hội An holding their own in between. The food is the architecture of the culture here: breakfast pho, banh mi from a cart, a bowl of bún bò Huế at a plastic table at noon.

cities.name: Hanoi
cities.notes: The old quarter's 36 streets, bia hơi corners, Hoan Kiem Lake at dawn. Cooler, older, and less immediately flashy than the south.

cities.name: Hội An
cities.notes: Lantern-lit old town, tailors, ancient trading port history. The food here — white rose dumplings, cao lầu — exists nowhere else.

cities.name: Ho Chi Minh City
cities.notes: Motorbikes everywhere, always. District 1 for history, District 3 for coffee, Bình Thành for produce markets at 5am.

cities.name: Hà Giang
cities.notes: The far north: rice terraces cut into limestone mountains, winding passes, almost no tourists. The most dramatic landscape in the country.

food.name: Phở
food.notes: The north's version is cleaner, more subtle — just broth, noodles, beef, herbs. Eaten for breakfast, always.

food.name: Bánh mì
food.notes: French baguette meets Vietnamese filling: pâté, pickled daikon, cilantro, chili. A civilizational achievement for under $1.

food.name: Bún bò Huế
food.notes: Hue's spicy lemongrass beef noodle soup — more complex and punchy than phở, and criminally underrated outside Vietnam.

food.name: Cơm tấm
food.notes: Broken rice with grilled pork, a fried egg, pickled vegetables. Saigon's default lunch, eaten at 11am at a plastic stool.

food.name: Egg coffee
food.notes: Hanoi's invention: robusta espresso topped with a thick, sweet egg yolk cream. Dessert and caffeine in one cup.

tips: The overnight train from Hanoi to Da Nang is worth taking — scenic coastal section in the morning.
Motorbike taxis (Grab Bike) are the fastest way through city traffic. Learn to love them.
Coffee culture is serious. Ca phe trung (egg coffee), ca phe sua da (iced milk coffee) — spend time in a small café.
Street food before 10am is often the best meal of the day. Follow where locals are eating.

name: Morocco
tagline: Labyrinth medinas, spiced tagines, and light that photographers chase forever.
description: Morocco operates on its own logic — the medinas are purposefully maze-like, the souks are intentionally overwhelming, and the pace shifts the moment you stop fighting it. The country layers Berber, Arab, and French influences in a way that makes the food, architecture, and daily life impossible to flatten into anything simple.

cities.name: Marrakech
cities.notes: Djemaa el-Fna at dusk — food stalls, smoke, drummers, storytellers. The medina at 7am before everything opens is equally compelling.

cities.name: Fes
cities.notes: The best-preserved medieval city in the world. The tanneries, the university, the 9,000 streets of the medina. A full day just to begin.

cities.name: Chefchaouen
cities.notes: The blue city in the Rif Mountains. The color is real, the hike up to the Spanish mosque is worth it, the kif is famously available.

cities.name: Sahara (Merzouga)
cities.notes: Dunes at sunrise, a night in a desert camp under a star sky that urban life has made you forget exists.

food.name: Tagine
food.notes: Slow-cooked in a conical clay pot — lamb with prunes and almonds, chicken with preserved lemon and olives. The technique is the flavor.

food.name: Couscous
food.notes: Friday's traditional meal: hand-rolled semolina, seven vegetables, a stew poured over the top. A communal ceremony.

food.name: Pastilla
food.notes: Pigeon (or chicken) in flaky warqa pastry, dusted with sugar and cinnamon. Sweet-savory and confusing in the best way.

food.name: Harira
food.notes: Tomato, lentil, and chickpea soup. The soup that breaks the Ramadan fast and feeds you on cold desert mornings.

food.name: Mint tea
food.notes: Gunpowder green tea, fresh mint, a theatrical pour from height to create foam. Hospitality in a glass.

tips: Hire a local guide for your first day in a medina — it's worth it to understand the logic before navigating alone.
Bargaining in souks is expected. Start at a third of the asking price and work up. The ritual is part of the transaction.
The train from Marrakech to Fes via Casablanca is comfortable and scenic.
Dress modestly outside of riads and tourist areas — shoulders and knees covered shows respect and improves how you're treated.

name: Portugal
tagline: Fado, custard tarts, and a melancholy beauty that gets under your skin.
description: Portugal has been in vogue for years now and still manages to feel unhurried — the country moves at a pace that makes you want to extend your trip by a week and read in a café for an afternoon without guilt. The food is simple and exceptional, the wine is criminally underpriced, and the light on the Alentejo plains in the evening is a particular shade of gold.

cities.name: Lisbon
cities.notes: Seven hills, vintage trams, fado drifting from open windows at night. Alfama at dusk. The best custard tart in the world at Pastéis de Belém.

cities.name: Porto
cities.notes: Ribeira's tiled facades, the Douro River, port wine caves across in Vila Nova de Gaia. A smaller, more intimate city than Lisbon.

cities.name: Alentejo
cities.notes: Cork trees, wheat plains, medieval walled towns, and some of the best red wine in Europe. The slow travel antidote.

cities.name: Algarve
cities.notes: The southern coast: limestone cliffs, hidden sea caves, and towns that haven't fully given in to the resorts.

food.name: Pastel de nata
food.notes: Custard tart in flaky pastry, dusted with cinnamon and powdered sugar. Best eaten warm at a stand-up counter with a bica (espresso).

food.name: Bacalhau
food.notes: Salt cod prepared 365 ways — this is not an exaggeration. À brás (with eggs and chips), com natas (with cream), cozido with vegetables.

food.name: Bifanas
food.notes: Marinated pork on a crusty roll. The Portuguese sandwich that makes everything else feel under-seasoned.

food.name: Grilled sardines
food.notes: Charcoal-grilled, eaten whole with bread and a glass of vinho verde. June's Santo António festival in Lisbon is the peak moment.

food.name: Francesinha
food.notes: Porto's legendary hangover sandwich: bread, meat, more meat, melted cheese, and a beer-and-tomato sauce poured over the top.

tips: Eat lunch at a restaurant with a menu do dia — set lunch of soup, main, and dessert for €10-12 is standard.
The Douro Valley by train is one of the great rail journeys in Europe. Pinhão is worth an overnight stop.
Lisbon's trams are scenic but slow. The Metro is fast. Learn both.
Vinho verde isn't just white — the red version is excellent and rarely exported. Drink it there.

name: Argentina
tagline: Steak, red wine, tango, and Patagonia's end-of-the-world drama.
description: Argentina is a country of superlatives and extremes — the world's greatest steakhouses in Buenos Aires, the otherworldly granite towers of Patagonia, the salt flats of Jujuy, and a wine country centered on Mendoza that gives Bordeaux a serious argument. The people are intense, opinionated, and exactly the kind of company you want over a long dinner.

cities.name: Buenos Aires
cities.notes: Paris crossed with chaos. Palermo's leafy streets, San Telmo's antique markets, La Boca's painted buildings. Dinner starts at 10pm and nobody finds this unusual.

cities.name: Mendoza
cities.notes: High-altitude malbec vineyards at the foot of the Andes. Bicycle between bodegas and eat an asado lunch under the grape vines.

cities.name: Bariloche
cities.notes: Lakes, peaks, Swiss-style chocolate shops, and the gateway to hiking Patagonia's northern circuit.

cities.name: El Calafate / El Chaltén
cities.notes: The Perito Moreno Glacier calves ice into the water. The Fitz Roy range at dawn in El Chaltén looks like a painting no one would believe.

food.name: Asado
food.notes: Not just a grill — a ritual. Hours of slow charcoal cooking, the parrillero tending the meat with the seriousness of a priest. Chorizo, morcilla, beef ribs, then the main cut.

food.name: Empanadas
food.notes: Baked or fried, each province with its own folding technique and filling. Saltenas in the north, humita in Mendoza. The filling code is in the crimping.

food.name: Malbec
food.notes: The grape Argentina made its own. High-altitude Mendoza versions are structured and extraordinary. Drink as much as you can while you're there.

food.name: Dulce de leche
food.notes: Caramelized milk spread on everything. In ice cream, on toast, inside alfajores. The country runs on it.

tips: Argentina's economy means exchange rates shift constantly — arrive informed about the best way to exchange currency.
Dinner genuinely starts at 9 or 10pm. Showing up at 7pm means eating alone in an empty restaurant.
Patagonia requires planning — trails, buses, and huts book out months in advance in high season.
Learn a few words of lunfardo (Buenos Aires slang) — it's not Spanish Spanish and locals appreciate the effort.

name: India
tagline: A civilization so dense and layered it takes multiple trips to even begin.
description: India is not a single experience — it's a subcontinent of contradictions operating simultaneously at full volume. The temples of Rajasthan, the Kerala backwaters, the Himalayan foothills, the chaos of Delhi, the calmness of Varanasi at dawn on the Ganga. Every region has its own cuisine, language, and emotional register, and a month barely touches it.

cities.name: Varanasi
cities.notes: One of the oldest cities on earth. The ghats at dawn, the evening aarti, the burning ghats — confrontational, sacred, unlike anywhere else.

cities.name: Jaipur
cities.notes: The Pink City: Amer Fort, Hawa Mahal, spice markets. The gateway to Rajasthan's desert forts and palaces.

cities.name: Kerala
cities.notes: Backwaters by houseboat, coconut-heavy cuisine, Ayurvedic treatments, tiger reserves. The slow south.

cities.name: Mumbai
cities.notes: The city that never sleeps, means it. Dharavi, Marine Drive at night, street food at Mohammed Ali Road, the art deco Oval Maidan.

food.name: Dosa
food.notes: Fermented rice and lentil crepe, crisp at the edges, with sambar and coconut chutney. South India's contribution to the world.

food.name: Biryani
food.notes: Every city swears its version is superior — Hyderabadi dum biryani, Lucknowi with its subtle spicing, Kolkata's with potatoes.

food.name: Thali
food.notes: A complete meal on one plate — dal, sabzi, rice, roti, pickle, papad. The logic of balance expressed through food.

food.name: Chaat
food.notes: Delhi street food: pani puri, bhel puri, aloo tikki. Sour, spicy, sweet, crunchy, all at once. Eaten standing up at a cart.

food.name: Masala chai
food.notes: Ginger, cardamom, black tea, milk, sugar. From a clay cup by the side of the road. The daily ritual that keeps the country moving.

tips: Book trains through IRCTC well in advance — the rail network is extraordinary but popular routes fill fast.
Stomach adjustment is real. Eat street food, but start gradually. Your gut needs a week to calibrate.
Remove shoes at temples, cover heads at Sikh gurudwaras, dress conservatively in religious sites.
The distance between two pins on a map tells you nothing about how long it takes to get between them.

name: New Zealand
tagline: Two islands, five climates, and landscapes that feel computer-generated.
description: New Zealand's scale is deceptive — you think you can cover it in two weeks and then you realize the South Island alone could take a month. The landscapes shift from fjords to glaciers to volcanic plains to golden beaches within a day's drive, and the hiking (tramping, they call it) is among the best and most accessible in the world.

cities.name: Queenstown
cities.notes: Adventure sports capital, Remarkables ski resort, Milford Sound day trips. Overhyped as a party town but extraordinary as a landscape base.

cities.name: Auckland
cities.notes: Volcanic island city. Sky Tower views, Waiheke Island wine day trip, Ponsonby's food scene.

cities.name: Christchurch
cities.notes: Rebuilt after the earthquake in unexpectedly creative ways — shipping container malls, guerrilla art, a city reimagining itself.

cities.name: Rotorua
cities.notes: Geothermal landscape: bubbling mud pools, geysers, sulfur in the air. The Māori cultural experience here is the most accessible in the country.

food.name: Hāngī
food.notes: Māori earth oven cooking — chicken, pork, kumara, and root vegetables steamed underground. The flavor is unlike anything cooked above ground.

food.name: Pavlova
food.notes: Meringue shell, whipped cream, kiwi and passionfruit on top. New Zealand and Australia both claim it. The debate is eternal.

food.name: Green-lipped mussels
food.notes: Enormous, fresh, eaten steamed with white wine and herbs. The best ones come from the South Island's Marlborough Sounds.

food.name: Flat white
food.notes: New Zealand's contribution to coffee culture — smaller than a latte, stronger, better ratio. The origin of a global category.

tips: Rent a campervan for the South Island — it's the best way to stop where the light is right and change plans freely.
Book Great Walk huts (Milford Track, Routeburn) months ahead. They sell out fast.
The drive from Queenstown to Milford Sound is best done in a day — go early, return for sunset over the lake.
Weather changes extremely fast, especially in the south. Layers even in summer.

name: Iceland
tagline: Midnight sun, northern lights, and a country built on fire and ice.
description: Iceland is geologically young and in constant motion — new land is still forming in the Reykjanes Peninsula and the glaciers that cover the interior are retreating fast enough to mark on a decade's timeline. The light in summer doesn't set, and in winter, on a clear night away from Reykjavik, the northern lights are the most quietly overwhelming thing in nature.

cities.name: Reykjavik
cities.notes: The world's northernmost capital. Small, walkable, colorful. Laugavegur street, Hallgrímskirkja church, the best hot dog in Europe at Bæjarins Beztu.

cities.name: Snæfellsnes Peninsula
cities.notes: Glacier volcano, lava fields, black sand beaches, and the lighthouse at the world's edge. Jules Verne's journey to the center of the earth starts here.

cities.name: Jökulsárlón
cities.notes: A glacial lagoon where icebergs calve off Vatnajökull and float to a black sand beach. The ice is blue. The seals sit on it.

cities.name: Landmannalaugar
cities.notes: Multicolored rhyolite mountains in the highland interior. The start of the Laugavegur Trek and one of the most alien landscapes on earth.

food.name: Skyr
food.notes: Not quite yogurt, not quite cheese. Thick, protein-dense, slightly sour. The base of every Icelandic breakfast for a thousand years.

food.name: Lamb
food.notes: Free-range on volcanic grass all summer. The meat is leaner and more complex than anything that was penned. Grilled, roasted, in lamb soup.

food.name: Plokkfiskur
food.notes: Fish stew: haddock or cod, potatoes, onion, béchamel. The kind of food that makes sense when it's 2°C and the wind is serious.

food.name: Rúgbrauð
food.notes: Dense rye bread, traditionally baked in geothermal ground. With Icelandic butter and smoked salmon, it's a remarkable thing.

tips: A rental car (ideally 4WD) is essential. Public transport doesn't reach the landscapes you came for.
The Ring Road (Route 1) is 1,300km and doable in 7–10 days. Don't rush the south coast.
Northern lights require clear skies and solar activity. Have a backup plan, be flexible.
The Blue Lagoon is a tourist trap. The Myvatn Nature Baths in the north are better, emptier, and less expensive.

name: Mexico
tagline: The country that invented corn, chocolate, and the world's most complex sauces.
description: Mexico's food alone justifies the trip — it is one of only a handful of national cuisines with UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage status, and eating your way through Oaxaca's moles, Mexico City's tacos al pastor, and Yucatán's cochinita pibil is enough reason to return repeatedly. The culture, archaeological sites, and Pacific and Caribbean coasts are everything else you didn't expect.

cities.name: Mexico City
cities.notes: Overwhelming, exciting, and one of the great food cities on earth. Roma and Condesa for brunch and natural wine, Coyoacán for Frida Kahlo, the Zócalo for everything.

cities.name: Oaxaca
cities.notes: The food and mezcal capital. Seven moles, tlayudas, memelas, chapulines. The Saturday market at Tlacolula is unmissable.

cities.name: Mérida
cities.notes: The Yucatán's cultural capital — colonial plazas, hammocks, cochinita pibil for Sunday breakfast, Chichen Itza a day trip away.

cities.name: San Cristóbal de las Casas
cities.notes: Highland Chiapas town surrounded by indigenous Maya communities. Amber, textiles, cold nights, and some of the best coffee in Mexico.

food.name: Tacos al pastor
food.notes: Pork marinated in achiote and dried chilis, carved from a vertical spit, served with pineapple and salsa on a corn tortilla. Mexico City's street masterpiece.

food.name: Mole negro
food.notes: Oaxaca's signature sauce: 30+ ingredients including chilis, chocolate, and charred tortilla. Hours to make, impossible to fully understand.

food.name: Cochinita pibil
food.notes: Slow-roasted pork marinated in bitter orange and achiote, wrapped in banana leaves, cooked underground. Yucatán's ancient technique.

food.name: Mezcal
food.notes: Oaxacan smoked agave spirit. Not tequila. Sipped slowly from a clay cup with an orange slice and sal de gusano.

food.name: Elote
food.notes: Grilled corn on a stick, covered in mayo, cheese, lime, and chile powder. The best street food in a country of extraordinary street food.

tips: Eat on the street. The taco stand that's been there for 30 years with the line out front will be better than the sit-down restaurant.
Mexico City's altitude (2,240m) and pollution can hit you the first day. Take it easy on arrival.
Oaxacan mezcal is not the same as what's exported. Drink papalometl or tobalá in a small mezcalería.
The overnight bus network is excellent, comfortable, and how locals travel between cities.

name: Thailand
tagline: Temples, street food, and an ocean the color of shallow water should not be.
description: Thailand has been a backpacker standard for decades and still delivers — the temples are extraordinary, the food is cheap and exceptional, and the islands are exactly as beautiful as everyone says. The north is a different country from the south: Chiang Mai's mountains, night bazaars, and cooking schools vs the Andaman coast's limestone karsts and turquoise bays.

cities.name: Bangkok
cities.notes: Chaotic, vibrant, exhausting in the best sense. Wat Pho at sunrise, Chatuchak market on weekends, rooftop bars at night, pad see ew from a cart at 2am.

cities.name: Chiang Mai
cities.notes: The north's cultural capital: Doi Inthanon national park, Sunday Walking Street, Thai cooking classes in the old city.

cities.name: Chiang Rai
cities.notes: The White Temple (Wat Rong Khun) is as strange as it looks in photos. The Golden Triangle and tea plantations of Doi Mae Salong nearby.

cities.name: Krabi / Railay
cities.notes: Limestone sea cliffs, long-tail boats, beaches only accessible by water. The standard for what a Thai beach is supposed to be.

food.name: Pad thai
food.notes: The export version is nothing like the real thing: rice noodles, egg, shrimp, tamarind, fish sauce, lime, and bean sprouts from a wok over high flame.

food.name: Som tum
food.notes: Green papaya salad pounded in a mortar: sour, spicy, sweet, salty. Adjustable heat. The most refreshing thing in 35°C heat.

food.name: Khao soi
food.notes: Northern Thai coconut curry noodle soup with crispy noodles on top. Chiang Mai's signature dish and one of the great bowls in Asia.

food.name: Mango sticky rice
food.notes: Sweet glutinous rice, fresh mango, coconut cream sauce. Seasonal — best from April to June when the mangoes are ripe.

food.name: Tom yum goong
food.notes: Spicy-sour prawn soup with lemongrass, galangal, kaffir lime leaves, chili. The flavor is bright and aggressive in exactly the right measure.

tips: Remove shoes at temple entrances, keep shoulders and knees covered. A sarong serves both purposes.
The sleeper train from Bangkok to Chiang Mai is comfortable, scenic, and a classic way to travel.
Street food from carts and market stalls is safe and often superior to restaurants. The chef cooks one thing and does it well.
Songkran (Thai New Year, April) means a nationwide water fight. Plan around it or join it.

name: Spain
tagline: Late dinners, afternoon siestas, and a country that takes pleasure seriously.
description: Spain runs on a schedule that seems designed to maximize enjoyment: coffee and toast at 9am, pintxos at noon, proper lunch at 2pm, siesta, evening vermouth at 7pm, dinner at 10pm, then wherever the night goes. Each region — Basque Country, Catalonia, Andalusia, Galicia — operates with its own food culture and barely disguised sense of superiority about it.

cities.name: San Sebastián
cities.notes: More Michelin stars per capita than anywhere on earth. The pintxos bars of the old town — Gilda, anchovy on pepper on olive — are the introduction.

cities.name: Barcelona
cities.notes: Gaudí's unfinished city: the Sagrada Família, Park Güell, Casa Batlló. La Boqueria for morning coffee, El Born for the afternoon.

cities.name: Seville
cities.notes: Flamenco, orange trees, the Alcázar, and tapas culture at its most essential. The city is best in spring when the jacaranda bloom.

cities.name: Granada
cities.notes: The Alhambra at dawn, the Albaicín neighborhood across the valley, and tapas that come free with every drink.

food.name: Pintxos
food.notes: Basque Country's bar snacks: bread with anchovy, Gilda skewers, croquetas, txistorra sausage. The ritual is standing, ordering wine, and reaching for the plate.

food.name: Jamón ibérico de bellota
food.notes: Acorn-fed Iberian pig, aged 36+ months. The best is from Extremadura or Huelva. Thin-sliced at room temperature — no cooking required.

food.name: Paella valenciana
food.notes: From Valencia, not Barcelona. Rice, rabbit, chicken, green beans, saffron. Cooked in a paella pan over orange wood. The socarrat (crispy bottom) is the goal.

food.name: Tortilla española
food.notes: Potato and egg, cooked slowly in olive oil. Best eaten at room temperature at a bar counter with a glass of wine at noon.

food.name: Churros con chocolate
food.notes: Fried dough dipped in thick drinking chocolate. Served at 3am after a night out. Spain's most important contribution to late-night culture.

tips: Eat on Spanish time. Lunch at 2pm, dinner at 9:30pm. Arriving at 7pm gets you an empty restaurant and bad energy.
The AVE high-speed train connects Madrid, Barcelona, Seville, and Málaga. Often faster than flying city center to city center.
In Granada, tapas come free with drinks. Drink slowly, eat a lot.
August in Madrid or Seville is genuinely brutal — 42°C and everything moves underground.

name: Norway
tagline: Fjords so still they look like mirrors, and a darkness that makes the light mean something.
description: Norway's landscape is a specific kind of extreme — the fjords cut hundreds of meters deep into cliffs that rise vertically above you, and the light in summer goes golden at midnight and stays that way for hours. The cities are expensive but the wilderness is free, and the country rewards the effort of getting into it properly rather than passing through.
best_time: May–September (fjords and midnight sun), November–February (northern lights)

cities.name: Bergen
cities.notes: Gateway to the fjords. Bryggen's Hanseatic wharf, the Fløibanen funicular for views, and fish soup from the market by the harbor.

cities.name: Flåm / Sognefjord
cities.notes: The world's deepest fjord. The Flåm Railway descends through waterfalls from the mountain plateau to the water — one of the great train rides in Europe.

cities.name: Tromsø
cities.notes: The Arctic city and the best base for northern lights in Norway. Dog sledding, reindeer, and the aurora over the fjord.

cities.name: Lofoten Islands
cities.notes: Fishing villages on dramatic rocky peaks surrounded by sea. The most photogenic place in Norway and possibly in Europe.

food.name: Smoked salmon
food.notes: Norwegian Atlantic salmon, cold-smoked and thinly sliced. With rye bread, dill cream, capers. The Scandinavian open sandwich is the delivery vehicle.

food.name: Rakfisk
food.notes: Fermented trout — Norway's answer to hákarl. Eaten at Christmas, divisive, genuinely ancient. Acquired taste worth acquiring.

food.name: Kjøttkaker
food.notes: Norwegian meatballs in brown gravy with mashed potato and lingonberry jam. Comfort food for long dark winters.

food.name: Brunost
food.notes: Brown cheese made from whey. Caramel-sweet, sliced thin on waffles or crispbread. A uniquely Norwegian morning.

tips: Norway is expensive. Budget significantly more than the rest of Europe — cook where you can, eat lunch as the main meal.
The Hurtigruten coastal ferry from Bergen to Kirkenes is one of the great slow journeys in the world.
The Norway in a Nutshell route (train, bus, ferry, train) from Oslo to Bergen is a classic for a reason.
Daylight in summer is relentless — bring a good sleep mask.

name: South Korea
tagline: Where hyper-modernity and deep tradition share the same street corner.
description: South Korea moves at a speed that is almost disorienting — a place that rebuilt itself entirely within living memory and then decided to export its culture to the rest of the world. Beneath the K-pop and tech economy is a country with centuries of ceramics, temple stays, and fermented everything. Seoul alone could keep you busy for a month.

cities.name: Seoul
cities.notes: Gangnam for the glass towers, Bukchon for the hanok alleys, Hongdae for the energy at midnight. All of it in one city.

cities.name: Busan
cities.notes: Coastal city with raw seafood markets at dawn, colorful hillside neighborhoods, and an entirely different tempo from Seoul.

cities.name: Gyeongju
cities.notes: Ancient Silla capital with royal tombs rising from the city floor like green hills. History you can walk through.

food.name: Korean BBQ
food.notes: The communal act of grilling meat at the table, with banchan accumulating around you. Samgyeopsal at midnight is a tradition.

food.name: Bibimbap
food.notes: Rice, vegetables, gochujang, and an egg — simple but perfectly balanced when done right.

food.name: Jjigae
food.notes: Bubbling stew that arrives still boiling at the table. Sundubu jjigae on a cold morning is unbeatable.

tips: T-money card works on all transit — get one at the airport.
Convenience stores (GS25, CU) are a legitimate meal option at any hour.
Learn a few phrases in Korean — the effort is genuinely appreciated.

name: Turkey
tagline: Two continents, one country, and ten thousand years of being at the center of everything.
description: Turkey sits at the hinge of civilizations — Byzantine, Ottoman, Greco-Roman, and Anatolian layers stacked so thick that digging for a new metro stop turns up another ancient city. Istanbul alone is an argument for staying a month. But then there is Cappadocia at sunrise and the turquoise Aegean and the southeast that most travelers never reach.

cities.name: Istanbul
cities.notes: Hagia Sophia, the Grand Bazaar, rooftop tea with a Bosphorus view — and then the neighborhoods: Karaköy, Beyoğlu, Balat.

cities.name: Cappadocia
cities.notes: Hot air balloons over fairy chimneys at first light. Underground cave cities carved by early Christians. Genuinely surreal.

cities.name: Göreme
cities.notes: Base for Cappadocia — stay in a cave hotel carved from the tufa rock. Worth every cliché.

food.name: Meze spread
food.notes: Dozens of small dishes — hummus, stuffed grape leaves, fried cheese, ezme — before the main event even arrives.

food.name: Kebap
food.notes: Adana, İskender, döner — each city has its own and will politely insist theirs is the real one.

food.name: Baklava
food.notes: Gaziantep is the capital of Turkish baklava. The pistachio version from the right shop will ruin all other desserts.

tips: Book Cappadocia balloon rides months ahead — they sell out constantly.
The Turkish bath (hamam) is worth doing properly at a historic one.
Bargaining is expected at the Grand Bazaar — enjoy the ritual.

name: Egypt
tagline: The oldest civilization on earth, and it still manages to surprise you.
description: Egypt is one of those places where you think you know it from photographs and then you stand in front of the pyramids and realize no image has ever come close. The scale is incomprehensible. Beyond Cairo there is Luxor's open-air museum of temples, the Valley of the Kings, the Nile's slow drift south, and a desert that goes on further than imagination allows.

cities.name: Cairo
cities.notes: Chaotic, loud, overwhelming, and completely alive. The Egyptian Museum, Khan el-Khalili, and the pyramids at Giza all in one day.

cities.name: Luxor
cities.notes: East bank for temples, west bank for the Valley of the Kings. Cycling between sites at dawn before the heat arrives.

cities.name: Aswan
cities.notes: Slower, quieter, and deeply Nubian. The Philae temple island at dusk is otherworldly.

food.name: Koshari
food.notes: Cairo street food — lentils, pasta, rice, fried onions, and tomato sauce. Looks like chaos, tastes like comfort.

food.name: Ful medames
food.notes: Fava bean breakfast eaten with flatbread everywhere. The Egyptian morning.

food.name: Grilled kofta
food.notes: Spiced ground meat on skewers, with tahini and fresh bread. Deceptively simple.

tips: Hire a local guide for Luxor — context turns ruins into stories.
Visit pyramids at Giza first thing in the morning, not midday.
Negotiate everything — it is part of the social contract, not an imposition.

name: Brazil
tagline: The largest country in South America and somehow still feels like it has been held back from you.
description: Brazil is so large it contains multitudes — the Amazon basin that holds a fifth of the world's freshwater, the otherworldly sand dunes of Lençóis Maranhenses, the Pantanal where wildlife viewing rivals any safari, and Rio de Janeiro, which is simply one of the most beautiful cities on earth in the most theatrical way possible.

cities.name: Rio de Janeiro
cities.notes: Ipanema and Copacabana are just the beginning — Lapa, Santa Teresa, the tram to Corcovado. The city is a film set.

cities.name: Salvador
cities.notes: Afro-Brazilian culture in its richest form — Carnaval here is older and wilder than Rio's.

cities.name: Manaus
cities.notes: Gateway to the Amazon. A 19th-century opera house in the middle of the jungle that still doesn't make sense.

food.name: Feijoada
food.notes: Black bean and pork stew served with rice, farofa, and orange slices. The national dish, eaten slowly on Saturdays.

food.name: Churrasco
food.notes: Brazilian BBQ done properly — endless skewers of different cuts brought to the table until you surrender.

food.name: Açaí
food.notes: Cold, thick, topped with granola and banana. Eaten as a meal, not a snack, especially in Pará where it originates.

tips: Learn basic Portuguese — English is not widely spoken outside tourist areas.
Book Amazon lodges far in advance for peak season.
Ubiquitous street food is usually the best food — trust the queue.

name: Australia
tagline: A continent at the edge of the world, quietly holding some of the most extraordinary landscapes on earth.
description: Australia rewards the traveler willing to go beyond the cities — though the cities themselves are excellent. The interior is ancient and vast in a way that resets something in the brain. The Great Barrier Reef is one of the last places on earth that makes you feel genuinely small. And the wildlife remains fundamentally alien to anyone who did not grow up with it.

cities.name: Sydney
cities.notes: Opera House and Harbour Bridge are the postcards, but Newtown and Glebe are the city. Bondi is as good as advertised.

cities.name: Melbourne
cities.notes: Coffee culture, laneways full of street art, farmers markets, and a food scene that punches above any global city.

cities.name: Uluru
cities.notes: The sandstone monolith at sunrise and sunset, as the color shifts from amber to deep red to violet. Cannot be photographed adequately.

food.name: Flat white
food.notes: Australia's contribution to coffee culture — Melbourne especially takes this seriously in a way that is contagious.

food.name: Barramundi
food.notes: Native fish, best grilled simply with lemon. Better at a coastal shack than anywhere fancy.

food.name: Meat pie
food.notes: Humble pastry filled with beef and gravy. Found everywhere, essential at a footy game.

tips: Distances are genuinely enormous — plan domestic flights, not drives.
Swim between the flags at beaches — rip currents are serious.
Respect Uluru — climbing is prohibited and the cultural significance is real.

name: Croatia
tagline: A coastline so absurdly beautiful it feels like someone was showing off.
description: Croatia packs an extraordinary amount into a relatively small country — medieval walled cities on the Adriatic, over a thousand islands, waterfalls in the interior, and a food and wine scene that most of Europe is only just catching up to. Dubrovnik is the famous one, but it is the lesser-known islands and the Dalmatian coast towns that tend to convert people.

cities.name: Dubrovnik
cities.notes: Walk the city walls at dawn before the cruise ships arrive. The limestone streets glow orange in the early light.

cities.name: Split
cities.notes: A Roman emperor's retirement palace that became a city — people still live inside Diocletian's Palace.

cities.name: Hvar
cities.notes: Lavender fields, Venetian architecture, and the kind of swimming spots that make you understand why people move here.

food.name: Black risotto
food.notes: Squid ink risotto from the Dalmatian coast — dramatic to look at, extraordinary to eat.

food.name: Peka
food.notes: Lamb or octopus slow-cooked under a bell-shaped lid covered in embers. Must be ordered the day before.

food.name: Pag cheese
food.notes: Sheep's milk cheese from the island of Pag, made from animals that graze on salt-blown herbs. Unique taste.

tips: Come in May or September — July and August are beautiful but genuinely crowded.
Rent a small boat to reach the hidden coves that ferries don't stop at.
Try Plavac Mali wine — Croatia's Dalmatian red that is underappreciated globally.

name: Nepal
tagline: Home to eight of the world's ten highest peaks, and somehow that undersells it.
description: Nepal sits at a kind of physical extreme — the Himalayas dominate everything, culturally and literally. Kathmandu is a dense, layered city where Hindu and Buddhist traditions overlap on every corner. The trekking routes that head north toward Everest base camp or into the Annapurna circuit are among the most extraordinary walks available to any human being willing to put in the altitude.

cities.name: Kathmandu
cities.notes: Durbar Square, Swayambhunath stupa at sunset, Thamel's organised chaos. Altitude hits at 1,400m — adjust first.

cities.name: Pokhara
cities.notes: Gateway to Annapurna, with Phewa Lake in the valley and Machhapuchhre dominating the skyline. A good place to breathe.

cities.name: Namche Bazaar
cities.notes: The Sherpa trading hub on the way to Everest — the last real town before the altitude becomes serious.

food.name: Dal bhat
food.notes: Lentil soup, rice, vegetable curry, and pickles. Eaten twice a day by most Nepalis, refillable, and honestly sustaining.

food.name: Momo
food.notes: Dumplings — steamed or fried, filled with meat or vegetables, served with a fiery tomato chutney. Everywhere.

food.name: Thukpa
food.notes: Noodle soup eaten at altitude. Simple, warming, exactly what you need after a long day of trekking.

tips: Acclimatize properly — altitude sickness is real and serious above 3,000m.
Hire a local guide and porter for trekking — it supports the community and they know the mountain.
Pack layers — temperature swings of 30°C between day and night are common.

name: Cuba
tagline: Frozen in a moment in time, and somehow more alive for it.
description: Cuba exists in a state that is hard to explain until you're standing in it — a place where the colonial architecture is genuinely crumbling and genuinely beautiful, where 1950s American cars serve as taxis, and where the music spills out of every window at every hour. Havana is one of the most photogenic cities on earth, but Trinidad and the tobacco valley of Viñales are the parts that tend to stay with you longest.

cities.name: Havana
cities.notes: Malecón at sunset, the old city on foot, mojitos at La Bodeguita del Medio — and then the real Havana, off the tourist map.

cities.name: Trinidad
cities.notes: A UNESCO colonial town frozen in the 18th century, with cobblestone streets and salsa playing from the stairs of every casa.

cities.name: Viñales
cities.notes: Tobacco farms in a valley of limestone mogotes. The cigars rolled by hand here are the ones worth bringing home.

food.name: Ropa vieja
food.notes: Shredded slow-cooked beef in tomato sauce — Cuba's national dish, best from a local paladar, not a hotel.

food.name: Tostones
food.notes: Twice-fried plantain, salted and served everywhere. The right accompaniment to almost anything.

food.name: Mojito
food.notes: Rum, lime, mint, sugar, soda — in Cuba, this is not a cocktail-menu item but a fact of life.

tips: Bring cash — US cards often don't work; exchange at a CADECA for the best rate.
Stay in casas particulares (private homestays) rather than state hotels for the real experience.
Download offline maps — internet access is limited and unreliable.

name: Switzerland
tagline: The Alps, but make it also fondue and watches and trains that run to the second.
description: Switzerland is almost aggressively beautiful — the kind of place that seems too composed to be real. The alpine landscapes are the obvious draw, but the cities are quietly excellent: Zürich for its lake and museums, Basel for its world-class art fairs, Geneva for the strange energy of a small city that hosts the planet's most serious conversations. And the food, which is better than its reputation suggests.

cities.name: Zürich
cities.notes: Lake Zürich, the old town, the Kunsthaus — and then the Bahnhofstrasse if you need to recalibrate your sense of wealth.

cities.name: Zermatt
cities.notes: Car-free village below the Matterhorn. The mountain appears from different angles all day and never gets ordinary.

cities.name: Lucerne
cities.notes: Chapel Bridge, the lake, the medieval walls — the most classically Swiss of the cities, in the best way.

food.name: Fondue
food.notes: Gruyère and Emmental melted with white wine in a communal pot. Best eaten in a mountain restaurant after hiking.

food.name: Rösti
food.notes: The Swiss-German answer to hash browns — crispy, buttery potato cake that accompanies almost everything.

food.name: Raclette
food.notes: Melted cheese scraped over potatoes, pickles, and onions. Simple and completely satisfying.

tips: The Swiss Travel Pass covers almost all trains, boats, and mountain railways — get it.
Book mountain railway tickets (Jungfraujoch, Gornergrat) in advance.
Budget generously — Switzerland is expensive in a way that still catches you off guard.

name: United Kingdom
tagline: Older than its accent suggests, weirder than its reputation allows.
description: The UK rewards going beyond London — though London itself is inexhaustible. Scotland is something else entirely: a country with its own parliament, its own weather, its own whisky, and landscapes that make the rest of Britain feel mild. Wales has mountains and coastline that most visitors never reach. But even London, properly explored by foot and Tube, still surprises.

cities.name: London
cities.notes: Borough Market, the Tate Modern, Soho at midnight, a Sunday roast in a proper pub. The city that contains every other city inside it.

cities.name: Edinburgh
cities.notes: The Old Town's medieval closes, Arthur's Seat at dusk, the castle on its volcanic rock. One of the most dramatic cities in Europe.

cities.name: Glasgow
cities.notes: Art Nouveau architecture, the best live music scene in the UK outside London, and food that has quietly improved enormously.

food.name: Full English breakfast
food.notes: Eggs, bacon, beans, black pudding, grilled tomato, toast. Best in a greasy spoon café, not a hotel.

food.name: Fish and chips
food.notes: Battered cod with proper chips, malt vinegar, and salt. Eaten from paper by the sea is the only correct setting.

food.name: Sunday roast
food.notes: Roast beef or chicken with Yorkshire pudding, roast potatoes, and gravy. The national ritual, best in a country pub.

tips: Get an Oyster card or contactless payment for London transit — never buy single tickets.
The National Rail is worth the effort to reach the countryside — day trips from London to Bath, Oxford, or the Cotswolds are easy.
Scottish whisky distilleries along the Speyside route offer tours worth building a trip around.

name: Canada
tagline: Vast, quietly breathtaking, and chronically underestimated as a travel destination.
description: Canada is the second-largest country on earth and most visitors see a tiny fraction of it. Quebec City feels genuinely European in a way that surprises people. British Columbia is one of the most beautiful places in the world. The Rockies around Banff and Jasper are the mountains that mountains aspire to be. And Toronto is a genuinely great city that tends to be overlooked next to Montreal.

cities.name: Montreal
cities.notes: The most European city in North America — French language, incredible food scene, underground city in winter, festivals all summer.

cities.name: Vancouver
cities.notes: Mountains, ocean, and old-growth forest within an hour of each other. The most naturally situated city on the continent.

cities.name: Quebec City
cities.notes: The only walled city in North America. The Old Town at dusk feels like France with better maple syrup.

cities.name: Banff
cities.notes: Turquoise glacial lakes, elk on the main street, and mountain views that feel excessive in the best way.

food.name: Poutine
food.notes: Fries, cheese curds, and gravy. Essential in Quebec, particularly at 2am. La Banquise in Montreal is the benchmark.

food.name: Montreal bagels
food.notes: Wood-fired, sweeter and denser than New York — the St-Viateur vs Fairmount debate is worth having in person.

food.name: Butter tarts
food.notes: Flaky pastry filled with a gooey butter-sugar filling. The Canadian dessert that the rest of the world has not discovered yet.

tips: VIA Rail trains between Montreal, Ottawa, Toronto, and Quebec City are scenic and worth taking once.
The national parks (Banff, Jasper, Algonquin) require advance planning in summer.
Quebec outside Montreal has very limited English — learning some French phrases is genuinely useful.

name: China
tagline: Five thousand years of civilization compressed into a country moving faster than any other.
description: China is too large and too complex to summarize — a place where a Song dynasty garden and a hypermodern bullet train station share the same city block, and where the food alone could justify a lifetime of travel. The scale of everything here is genuinely different from anywhere else: the rivers, the mountains, the cities, the history, the population. The key is choosing a region and going deep.

cities.name: Beijing
cities.notes: The Forbidden City, the hutongs at dusk, Peking duck from Quanjude, the Great Wall at Mutianyu before the crowds. The imperial capital.

cities.name: Shanghai
cities.notes: The Bund at night across from Pudong's skyline — two centuries of architecture facing each other across the Huangpu. Restless, global, excellent food.

cities.name: Chengdu
cities.notes: The capital of Sichuan cuisine and the place to see giant pandas. A slower, more comfortable pace than the eastern megalopolises.

cities.name: Guilin
cities.notes: Karst limestone peaks rising from rice fields and the Li River — the landscape that fills Chinese classical paintings. Genuinely looks like that.

food.name: Peking duck
food.notes: Crispy lacquered skin wrapped with pancake, spring onion, and hoisin. An elaborate ceremony worth doing properly in Beijing.

food.name: Sichuan hotpot
food.notes: Numbingly spicy broth with every ingredient imaginable. The communal ritual that defines Chengdu evenings.

food.name: Dim sum
food.notes: The Cantonese tradition of small plates with tea — best done Sunday morning in a loud, chaotic restaurant with a trolley.

tips: Download WeChat and set up Alipay before arriving — cash is increasingly obsolete in cities.
VPN is essential for accessing Google, Instagram, and most Western services.
The high-speed rail network is extraordinary — use it between cities instead of flying.

name: Germany
tagline: More interesting than its reputation, more layered than its stereotypes, and impossibly good at bread.
description: Germany rewards the traveler willing to look past the obvious. Berlin is one of the most alive and culturally dense cities in Europe — still shaped by its division in ways that are visible on every block. Munich is the fairy tale version of Germany, compact and excellently fed. The Rhine valley, the Black Forest, and the Baltic coast are landscapes most visitors never reach. And the Christmas markets are not a cliché — they are genuinely wonderful.

cities.name: Berlin
cities.notes: The East Side Gallery, Museum Island, Kreuzberg's food scene, the memorial at Checkpoint Charlie, and a club culture unlike any other city on earth.

cities.name: Munich
cities.notes: Marienplatz, the English Garden, the Deutsches Museum, and Oktoberfest — but also a city worth exploring in any month.

cities.name: Hamburg
cities.notes: The port city with the best fish market in Europe (Sunday, 5am), the Speicherstadt warehouse district, and an underrated dining scene.

cities.name: Cologne
cities.notes: The cathedral that took 632 years to finish, the Rhine promenade, and the base for exploring the Rhineland wine country.

food.name: Bread
food.notes: Germany has over 300 varieties of bread. A bakery breakfast of Brötchen with butter is a daily ritual that quickly becomes a craving.

food.name: Currywurst
food.notes: Berlin street food — sliced sausage with curried ketchup. Humble, iconic, best from a Imbiss stand with a paper plate.

food.name: Pretzels and beer
food.notes: Specifically in Bavaria, specifically in a beer garden, specifically with a Maß (one liter). The context matters enormously.

tips: The Deutsche Bahn rail network connects everything — book in advance for the cheapest fares.
Berlin is cheap by European capital standards — give yourself more time than you think you need.
Museum Island in Berlin requires a full day; buy the combined ticket.

name: Singapore
tagline: A city-state that turned humidity, ambition, and hawker stalls into an art form.
description: Singapore shouldn't work as well as it does — a tiny island that built a first-world metropolis from scratch in a single generation, then kept going. The food alone justifies the trip: Michelin-starred hawker stalls where the queue starts at 10am for a $3 bowl of chicken rice that will rearrange your understanding of what simple food can be.

cities.name: Chinatown
cities.notes: Maxwell Food Centre and Chinatown Complex are hawker hall of fame territory. Tian Tian Hainanese chicken rice has had a Michelin star. Come hungry.

cities.name: Little India
cities.notes: Mustafa Centre open 24 hours, the Sri Veeramakaliamman Temple blazing with color, and banana-leaf meals that cost almost nothing.

cities.name: Gardens by the Bay
cities.notes: The Supertrees are cheesy until you're standing underneath them at night with the light show playing and you realize they absolutely work.

food.name: Hainanese chicken rice
food.notes: Poached chicken over seasoned rice with three dipping sauces. Humble ingredients, relentlessly precise execution. Singapore's national dish for good reason.

food.name: Chili crab
food.notes: Mud crab in a thick, sweet-savory tomato-chili sauce. You need the fried mantou buns to mop it up. Messy, expensive, absolutely worth it.

food.name: Kaya toast and soft-boiled eggs
food.notes: The classic kopitiam breakfast — charcoal toast with coconut jam and butter, eggs cracked into a bowl with soy sauce. Start every morning this way.

food.name: Laksa
food.notes: Rich coconut curry noodle soup with prawns and fish cake. Katong laksa is the local style — spoon-only, the noodles cut short.

tips: The MRT is excellent and covers most of the city — get an EZ-Link card at any station.
Hawker centres are the move for every meal: cheap, delicious, and deeply local.
Changi Airport is legitimately worth arriving early for — Jewel's indoor waterfall is a spectacle.

name: Netherlands
tagline: Canals, bikes, tulip fields, and museums that hold some of the world's greatest paintings.
description: The Netherlands is smaller than you think and more beautiful than you expect. Amsterdam rewards slow exploration — leave the main drag and you'll find neighborhood cafes, indie bookshops, and canal-side benches where locals actually sit. But the country beyond Amsterdam is just as compelling: windmills, cheese markets, and a coast that gets dramatically wild in autumn.

cities.name: Amsterdam
cities.notes: The Rijksmuseum for Vermeer and Rembrandt, the Anne Frank House (book weeks ahead), the Jordaan neighborhood for aimless wandering, and a canal at golden hour.

cities.name: Utrecht
cities.notes: Often skipped but shouldn't be — double-decker canals with cafes at water level, a smaller old town that's easier to absorb than Amsterdam.

cities.name: Keukenhof
cities.notes: Only open March–May, but if you're there for tulip season, the garden near Lisse is a genuine spectacle. 7 million bulbs in bloom.

food.name: Stroopwafel
food.notes: Two thin waffle wafers with caramel syrup in the middle. Buy them fresh from a market stall and hold them over your coffee cup to let the steam warm the caramel.

food.name: Herring (haring)
food.notes: Raw salt-cured herring eaten from a stand with raw onion and pickles. The Dutch eat it by tilting their head back and lowering it in. Try it.

food.name: Rijsttafel
food.notes: Indonesian 'rice table' — the colonial legacy that became a Dutch institution. Dozens of small dishes served alongside rice. An entire meal and a history lesson.

tips: Rent a bike immediately — the Netherlands has 35,000km of bike paths and cycling is genuinely the best way to see the country.
Book the Anne Frank House online well in advance; walk-in tickets sell out within minutes of release.
The Dutch national museum card (Museumkaart) pays for itself in a day if you're visiting more than two museums.

name: United States
tagline: A country too big to ever fully know
description: Vast and contradictory — endless road trips, national parks, cities with their own distinct personalities. The kind of place you can spend a lifetime exploring without ever seeing it all.

cities.name: New York City
cities.notes: Already home — but still endlessly explorable.

cities.name: New Orleans
cities.notes: Jazz, food, and a city that refuses to be ordinary.

cities.name: Portland
cities.notes: Pacific Northwest vibes, coffee, and the coast nearby.

cities.name: Savannah
cities.notes: Spanish moss, historic squares, and slow Southern time.

food.name: Nashville Hot Chicken
food.notes: The original, not a chain imitation.

food.name: New Orleans Gumbo
food.notes: A bowl of everything that makes this city special.

food.name: Tacos (Tex-Mex border)
food.notes: Drive the border towns for the real thing.

tips: Drive whenever possible — the landscape between destinations is half the experience.
Each region is essentially its own country. The South, Pacific Northwest, and Southwest feel entirely different.
National parks require planning — permits sell out months in advance for popular spots.

name: Peru
tagline: A lost city in the clouds and a civilization that built it.
description: Peru is one of the most dramatic countries on Earth — Machu Picchu alone would justify the trip, but there's also the Amazon basin, the floating islands of Lake Titicaca, and a food scene in Lima that has quietly become one of the best on the planet. The Inca Trail is a pilgrimage that rewards every step.

cities.name: Cusco
cities.notes: The former Inca capital at 3,400m — acclimatize here before the trail. Cobblestone streets, colonial churches built over Inca foundations, and excellent ceviche.

cities.name: Lima
cities.notes: One of the world's great food cities. Miraflores and Barranco for clifftop restaurants, ceviche at the market, pisco sours at sunset over the Pacific.

cities.name: Arequipa
cities.notes: The White City — built from volcanic sillar stone, framed by three volcanoes. Quieter than Cusco and a base for trekking Colca Canyon.

food.name: Ceviche
food.notes: Raw fish cured in lime juice with red onion and ají amarillo. The Peruvian version is the original and still the best — eaten at midday, never at night.

food.name: Lomo saltado
food.notes: Stir-fried beef with tomatoes, onions, and French fries, served over rice. The Chinese-Peruvian chifa fusion that became a national staple.

food.name: Causa rellena
food.notes: Layered yellow potato terrine filled with avocado and chicken or tuna. A deceptively elegant dish that shows up everywhere from street stalls to fine dining.

tips: Book Machu Picchu tickets online well in advance — entry is timed and limited, and sells out months ahead.
Altitude sickness is real above 3,000m. Spend 2–3 days in Cusco acclimatizing before the Inca Trail. Coca tea helps.
Lima's food scene is world-class — book Central, Maido, or Astrid y Gastón weeks ahead; walk-ins are almost impossible.

name: Colombia
tagline: Color, coffee, cumbia, and a country that refuses to be defined by its past.
description: Colombia has transformed dramatically and now ranks among South America's most exciting destinations. Cartagena's walled colonial city glows at golden hour, Medellín reinvented itself into a model of urban innovation, and the coffee region (Eje Cafetero) is one of the most beautiful landscapes on earth. The warmth of the people matches the warmth of the climate.

cities.name: Cartagena
cities.notes: A walled Caribbean city of bougainvillea-draped balconies, horse-drawn carriages, and rooftop bars. The old town at dusk is one of Latin America's great sights.

cities.name: Medellín
cities.notes: The city that reinvented itself. Cable cars over barrios, botanical gardens, and a nightlife scene that rivals anywhere on the continent.

cities.name: Salento
cities.notes: The heart of the coffee region — steep hills, wax palms in the Cocora Valley, and fincas where you can watch coffee go from cherry to cup.

food.name: Bandeja paisa
food.notes: The Antioquian mega-platter: red beans, rice, chicharrón, ground beef, chorizo, egg, avocado, and arepa. An entire day's calories in one enormous serving.

food.name: Arepas
food.notes: Griddled corn cakes eaten at every meal. Each region has its own style — Bogotá's plain and buttered, Costena's stuffed with cheese and egg.

food.name: Aguardiente
food.notes: Anise-flavored sugarcane spirit — the national drink, consumed in shots at any celebration. Sweet, strong, and an acquired taste that comes fast.

tips: Register with your country's embassy before arrival and stay updated on regional safety advisories — some areas remain off-limits.
The coffee region (Eje Cafetero) is best explored by jeep — book a guided tour from Salento for the Cocora Valley wax palms.
Colombian Spanish is considered among the clearest in Latin America — a good place to practice if you're learning the language.

name: Chile
tagline: The end of the world is a national park, and it's worth every kilometre.
description: Chile stretches over 4,300km from the driest desert on Earth in the north to the glaciers and granite spires of Patagonia in the south — one of the most geographically extreme countries anywhere. Torres del Paine is a trekking destination that defines bucket lists, the Atacama offers stargazing unlike anything in the northern hemisphere, and Santiago punches well above its weight as a city.

cities.name: Santiago
cities.notes: Backed by snow-capped Andes, with a thriving food and wine scene in Lastarria and Barrio Italia. Better than its reputation — spend a few days before heading south.

cities.name: Puerto Natales
cities.notes: The gateway town for Torres del Paine. Stock up on supplies, eat lamb stew by a wood-burning stove, and start the W Trek from here.

cities.name: San Pedro de Atacama
cities.notes: Desert oasis and stargazing capital. The Valle de la Luna at sunset, geysers at 4am, and salt flats that stretch to Bolivia.

food.name: Asado
food.notes: Chilean barbecue — whole lamb slow-roasted over an open fire in Patagonia. Found at every roadside parilla and perfected at the end of a long trekking day.

food.name: Empanadas de pino
food.notes: Baked pastries stuffed with ground beef, hard-boiled egg, olive, and raisin. The national snack, sold at every bakery and market stall.

food.name: Carménère
food.notes: Chile's signature red grape — thought extinct in Europe, it thrives in the Central Valley. Rich, smoky, and one of the world's best-value wines.

tips: Book Torres del Paine accommodation and camping spots 6–12 months ahead — the park fills up completely in high season (December–February).
Renting a car in Chile unlocks the country. The Carretera Austral is one of the world's great road trips — 1,240km of gravel through fjords and forest.
The Atacama's altitude (2,400m) hits suddenly after flying in from sea level. Take the first day slow.

name: South Africa
tagline: Table Mountain, the Big Five, and a wine route that rivals anywhere in the world.
description: South Africa is one of the most geographically and culturally diverse countries on Earth — a single trip can include world-class wildlife in Kruger, the dramatic coastline of the Garden Route, and Cape Town, which regularly tops lists of the world's most beautiful cities. The food, wine, and outdoor culture are all exceptional, and the light in the Western Cape is unlike anywhere else.
language: Zulu, Xhosa, Afrikaans, English (11 official languages)

cities.name: Cape Town
cities.notes: Table Mountain looming over everything, the V&A Waterfront, Bo-Kaap's pastel houses, and a 45-minute drive to some of the world's best wine estates.

cities.name: Johannesburg
cities.notes: The economic capital and gateway to Kruger. Soweto township tours, the Apartheid Museum, and a food and art scene in Maboneng worth a full day.

cities.name: Knysna
cities.notes: The jewel of the Garden Route — a lagoon flanked by forest, oyster farms, and hiking trails in the Tsitsikamma section of the national park.

food.name: Braai
food.notes: South African barbecue — a cultural institution more than a meal. Boerewors sausage, lamb chops, pap, and chakalaka around an open fire, for hours.

food.name: Bobotie
food.notes: Cape Malay spiced minced meat baked under a savory egg custard. A legacy of the Cape's spice trade history and one of the country's true national dishes.

food.name: Biltong
food.notes: Air-dried spiced meat — beef or game, cut thick or thin. Every shop sells it; every South African takes it on road trips. Addictively good.

tips: Rent a car — public transport is limited outside cities and driving gives you the Garden Route, the wine lands, and Cape Point on your own schedule.
Book Kruger self-drive camps (rest camps) well ahead, especially June–August. Early morning and dusk drives are when the Big Five are most active.
The Cape Doctor wind blows strong November–January. Visit Cape Town in autumn (March–May) for the best weather and fewest crowds.

name: Kenya
tagline: The wildebeest migration is the greatest wildlife spectacle on earth.
description: Kenya is the safari standard against which all others are measured. The Maasai Mara hosts the annual wildebeest migration, the most dramatic wildlife event on the planet, and the country's parks hold every major African species at close range. Beyond the animals, there's Nairobi's excellent restaurant scene, the Swahili coast around Lamu, and the culture of the Maasai people.

cities.name: Nairobi
cities.notes: A genuine city with a food scene that surprises — Carnivore restaurant for game meat, the Giraffe Centre, and Nairobi National Park literally on the city's edge.

cities.name: Masai Mara
cities.notes: The game reserve that defines East African safari. July to October for the crossing — hundreds of thousands of wildebeest fording the Mara River through crocodiles.

cities.name: Lamu
cities.notes: A UNESCO-listed Swahili island town with no cars, donkeys for transport, and some of the most atmospheric seafood restaurants in Africa.

food.name: Nyama choma
food.notes: Roasted goat meat — Kenya's national dish. Eaten with ugali (maize porridge) and kachumbari (tomato-onion salsa) at roadside joints called nyama spots.

food.name: Ugali
food.notes: The starchy backbone of Kenyan cooking — stiff maize porridge eaten with the right hand, used to scoop stews, beans, and sukuma wiki (collard greens).

food.name: Chai
food.notes: Kenyan chai is brewed strong with milk from the start — thick, sweet, and spiced. Available everywhere, always hot, drunk at all hours.

tips: Combine Kenya with Tanzania for the ultimate East Africa safari — cross into the Serengeti via the Mara crossing and follow the migration.
Go with a reputable guide or safari operator — it transforms the experience. Self-drive in the Mara is possible but having a tracker makes all the difference.
Malaria prophylaxis is strongly recommended for game reserve areas. Yellow fever vaccination is required if arriving from certain countries.

name: Jordan
tagline: Petra was carved from rose-red rock two thousand years ago and still stops you cold.
description: Jordan is one of the Middle East's most accessible and rewarding destinations — a stable, welcoming country holding some of antiquity's greatest monuments. Petra is among the world's true wonders; Wadi Rum is Lawrence of Arabia's desert come to life; and the Dead Sea lets you float without trying. The food is exceptional and the hospitality is genuine.

cities.name: Petra
cities.notes: Walk the Siq at dawn before the crowds. The Treasury reveals itself around the final bend — an almost theatrical moment. Spend two full days to see the whole site.

cities.name: Amman
cities.notes: A city of hills (jabals), Roman ruins, excellent hummus at Hashem restaurant, and a rooftop bar view over minarets at sunset.

cities.name: Wadi Rum
cities.notes: Vast red desert valleys, Bedouin camps under a sky full of stars, and Jeep tours through rock formations named after Lawrence of Arabia. Sleep in a camp — worth it.

food.name: Mansaf
food.notes: Jordan's national dish — slow-cooked lamb in fermented dried yogurt sauce (jameed) served over rice with almonds and pine nuts. Eaten communally by hand.

food.name: Mezze
food.notes: The spread: hummus, mutabbal, fattoush, tabbouleh, kibbeh, stuffed grape leaves. In Jordan the hummus is always warm and served with a pool of olive oil.

food.name: Knafeh
food.notes: A hot dessert of shredded pastry or semolina layered over stretchy white cheese, soaked in sugar syrup, and topped with crushed pistachios. Ordered from street carts.

tips: Buy the Jordan Pass before arrival — it covers the visa fee plus entry to Petra (2 days) and 40+ other sites. It pays for itself easily.
Petra at night (3 evenings per week) is a candlelit walk to the Treasury. Touristy but genuinely moving — worth doing in addition to the daytime visit.
Wadi Rum is a day trip from Petra but staying overnight is transformative — the silence and star density in the desert are extraordinary.

name: Sri Lanka
tagline: Temples, tea, elephants, and coastline — a small island with an improbable amount of everything.
description: Sri Lanka is compact enough to see a remarkable amount in two weeks but dense enough to reward a month. The Cultural Triangle holds some of Buddhism's most sacred sites; the central highlands have a tea industry that perfumed an empire; the south coast has some of Asia's best surfing; and the wildlife — especially leopards in Yala and elephants everywhere — rivals much larger countries.

cities.name: Kandy
cities.notes: Home of the Temple of the Sacred Tooth Relic — one of Buddhism's most important shrines. Set on a lake in the central highlands with daily puja ceremonies.

cities.name: Sigiriya
cities.notes: A 5th-century rock fortress rising 200m above the jungle, with frescoes and water gardens at the top. One of the most dramatic archaeological sites in Asia.

cities.name: Galle
cities.notes: A Dutch colonial fort town on the south coast — whitewashed ramparts, a lighthouse, boutique hotels in colonial buildings, and excellent cafes.

food.name: Rice and curry
food.notes: The national meal — a plate of rice surrounded by multiple small curries, sambol (coconut relish), and papadums. Every family has its own versions; all are good.

food.name: Hoppers (appa)
food.notes: Bowl-shaped fermented rice flour pancakes, eaten for breakfast — plain, with an egg cracked in the center, or with string hoppers and pol sambol.

food.name: Ceylon tea
food.notes: The island produces some of the world's finest black tea. Drink it in a hill-country bungalow with milk, watching mist roll over tea bushes at dawn.

tips: Trains between Kandy and Ella are among the world's most scenic — book the observation car (Expo Rail) in advance for the best views over tea country.
Tuk-tuks are the preferred way to get around towns. Negotiate a fair price before getting in and agree on it in writing if it's a longer trip.
Book Yala National Park safaris in the morning — leopard sightings are highest before 9am, and the park gets crowded with jeeps later in the day.

name: Indonesia
tagline: 17,000 islands, and Bali is just one of them.
description: Indonesia is a world unto itself — the fourth most populous country on Earth, stretching across an archipelago larger than the continental United States, containing some of the planet's oldest rainforests, most active volcanoes, and richest coral reefs. Bali is the gateway and still extraordinary, but Java's temples, Lombok's mountains, and Komodo's dragons reward those who push further.
